production.title: Production
production.content: According to data from the Japan Plastics Industry Federation, {{country}} produced approximately 9.5 million tonnes of plastics in primary forms (resins) and 5.6 million tonnes of plastic products in 2022. Among finished plastic products, plastic films and sheets account for the largest share at 43%, followed by plastic containers at 16% and plastic products for machine tools and parts at 12%. Within the films and sheets category, packaging materials account for approximately 20% of total production. When combined, plastic film packaging and plastic containers account for approximately 36% of {{country}}'s overall plastic product output, underscoring the dominant role of packaging applications in the country's plastics manufacturing sector.

trade.title: Trade
keyTrends: {{country}} remained a net exporter of plastics from 2017 to 2022, with exports averaging USD 41–46 billion and imports averaging USD 25–30 billion per year.
The trade surplus narrowed from about USD 15.6 billion (2017) to about USD 12.2 billion (2022).
{{country}} is a major exporter of primary plastics and semi-finished plastic products in the region, supporting downstream plastic manufacturing in other Asian countries.
The plastic waste trade is small relative to the trade in polymers and products, and waste exports declined substantially during 2017–2022, possibly due to China’s ban on plastic waste imports.
trade.summaryLeft:
Plastic Imports

A total of 164,333 million USD worth of plastic was imported to {{country}} between 2017-2022. In the latest reporting year 2022, the total imports reached value of 29,634 million USD, of which:

  • ~ 27% was plastic in primary forms
  • ~ 12% was plastic in intermediate forms
  • ~ 5% was intermediate manufactured plastic goods
  • ~ 56% was final manufactured plastic goods
  • ~ 0% was plastic waste.

The plastic imports included estimated 4,149 million USD of plastic packaging imports with notoriously short product lifetime.

Plastic exports

A total of 250,861 million USD worth of plastic was exported from {{country}} in the year(s) 2017-2022. In the latest reporting year 2022, exports reached a value of USD 41,864 million, of which:

  • ~ 36% was plastic in primary forms
  • ~ 28% was plastic in intermediate forms
  • ~ 7% was intermediate manufactured plastic goods
  • ~ 29% was final manufactured plastic goods
  • ~ 1% was plastic waste.

Plastic exports included estimated 6,280 million USD of plastic packaging.
trade.summary:
Plastic trade trends

Between years 2017 and 2022, the amount of plastic imported to {{country}} has increased by 16.3%, while the amount of plastic exported from {{country}} has increased by 2.0%.

{{country}}'s output volume of plastic products accounted for around 3% of global production in 2022. Between 2017 and 2022, {{country}}'s plastics economy remained strongly export-oriented and upstream-to-midstream-focused, with total plastic exports consistently exceeding imports each year. Over this period, total plastic exports increased from approximately USD 41 billion in 2017 to USD 41.9 billion in 2022, while imports rose more modestly from USD 25.5 billion to USD 29.6 billion, maintaining a substantial trade surplus.

Exports of primary plastics remained high throughout the period, peaking in 2021 before easing slightly in 2022. Export values consistently exceeded imports of primary plastics by a wide margin, confirming {{country}}'s continued strength in polymer production and supply, supported by its robust petrochemical infrastructure. A similar pattern holds for intermediate plastic forms and goods, where exports significantly outpaced imports every year, reflecting {{country}}'s role as a key supplier of semi-processed materials to global manufacturers.

Trade in final manufactured plastic goods was relatively stable, with both imports and exports remaining in the 11–16 billion range. Imports of final goods were consistently higher than exports, indicating that, although {{country}} produces and exports advanced plastic products, it also relies on imports of certain finished consumer and industrial goods.

The plastic waste trade remained marginal in value relative to primary and manufactured plastics. Waste exports declined steadily between 2017 and 2022, while imports of plastic waste stayed negligible, signalling a gradual reduction in reliance on waste exports during this period.
trade.tradingPartners:
Asia is by far {{country}}'s most important plastics trading partner on both the import and export side. Imports from Asia account for most of {{country}}'s total plastic imports, reflecting deep regional supply chain integration and {{country}}'s reliance on neighbouring Asian producers for certain plastic materials and products. On the export side, Asia is also the dominant destination, absorbing the largest share of {{country}}'s plastic exports, which is consistent with {{country}}'s role as a key upstream and midstream supplier within Asian manufacturing networks.

The Americas and Europe form a second tier of trading partners. {{country}} exports substantial volumes of plastics to both regions, particularly the Americas, indicating strong demand for Japanese polymers, intermediate materials, and manufactured plastic goods. Imports from these regions are much smaller, suggesting that trade with the Americas and Europe is primarily export-oriented rather than balanced.

Oceania and Africa play minor roles in {{country}}'s plastics trade. Both imports from and exports to these regions are small relative to those from and to Asia, Europe, and the Americas, indicating limited integration into {{country}}'s plastics value chain beyond niche or regional market linkages.
trade.productCategories:
{{country}}'s 2022 HS trade structure shows a strong upstream and mid-stream position in the global plastics value chain. The country is competitive in both primary polymers and high-quality semi-processed materials, with exports of plastic packaging exceeding imports.

The World Trade Organisation's database can provide more in-depth insights into specific products. Under plastics and articles thereof (HS Chapter 39), {{country}} predominantly imports unspecified plastic articles, along with a mix of primary polymers and packaging articles, indicating that, while domestic resin production is strong, it is not fully self-sufficient across all polymer types and grades. Imports of packaging and other plastic articles suggest selective reliance on foreign suppliers for certain finished or standardised products, likely driven by cost, scale, or regional supply-chain considerations. Exports are led by plastic sheets, films, and foils, followed by advanced resins such as polycarbonates, polyesters, acrylics, silicones, and vinyl polymers.

Synthetic textiles, apparel and footwear (HS chapters 54-65) represent {{country}}'s largest plastic-related import category. Imports are dominated by finished garments and made-up textile articles, led by knitted outerwear, women's and men's woven apparel, T-shirts, and footwear, indicating limited reliance on domestic mass garment assembly. Exports, by contrast, focus on upstream inputs, including synthetic filament yarn, woven fabrics, and synthetic staple fibres, with only minor exports of finished apparel.

{{country}} is a clear net exporter of rubber products (HS Chapter 40). Imports are predominantly key elastomer inputs (synthetic rubber and compounded rubber) and large volumes of finished tyres, indicating strong domestic demand and limited access to upstream feedstocks. At the same time, {{country}} exports substantial volumes of tyres and engineered rubber products, such as conveyor and transmission belts, hoses, and other vulcanised rubber articles.

consumption.title: Consumption
consumption.content: {{country}}'s plastic consumption estimates vary from 9-12.8 million tonnes in 2022, depending on methods and categories applied by different studies, where the dominant applications include construction (35%), packaging (27%), consumer products (9%), textiles (8%), transport (4%), and electronics and machinery (4%).

wasteManagement.title: Waste Management
keyTrends: {{country}}'s plastic waste is high per capita but stable in total volume, at approximately 9 million tonnes/year (2019-2022), reflecting a mature, high-consumption economy with limited growth in waste tonnage.

{{country}}'s plastic waste stream consists mostly of recyclable polymers and fibres, led by PP, LDPE, and polyester textiles, but these materials are not necessarily recycled in practice; the domestic plastic recycling rate was estimated at ~14% (~1.3 million tonnes) in 2022.

{{country}}'s heavy reliance on incinerating plastic waste and synthetic textiles in municipal solid waste results in GHG emissions of 8.3 million tonnes of CO2, indicating that {{country}}'s key climate change mitigation action lies in reducing plastic waste generation.
wasteManagement.content:
Plastic waste from municipal solid waste

An estimated 40,977 thousand tonnes of municipal solid waste were generated in {{country}} in 2020. According to a study by {{country}}'s Plastic Waste Management Institute, plastic waste generation from municipal solid waste was approximately 4.2 million tonnes in 2022 (0.09kg/person/day), of which 68% is incinerated with or without energy recovery or burned in cement kilns, 16% is mechanically recycled, 7% is chemically recycled and 3.5% is landfilled.

A study on global waste management modeling showed 100% of municipal solid waste is collection coverage, with annual plastic emission including both debris and open burning is 6,185 tonnes per year, which is equivalent to 0 kg/person/year. Estimated proportion of the MSW that is being recycled was 19.6% in year 2022.

The estimated MSW generation per capita in {{country}} in 2020 was 0.90 kg per person per day.

{{country}} relies heavily on incineration, with ~79% (7.1 Mt) of MSW burned, ~6% (0.5 Mt) landfilled, and <1% (0.1 Mt) mismanaged. Heavy reliance on incinerating plastic waste and synthetic textiles in municipal solid waste results in GHG emissions of 3.6 million tonnes of CO₂, indicating that {{country}}'s key climate change mitigation action lies in reducing plastic waste generation.
wasteManagement.contentRight:
Plastic waste from non-municipal solid waste

According to a study by {{country}}'s Plastic Waste Management Institute, 4.05 million tonnes of plastic waste from non-municipal solid waste were generated in 2022, of which 62% is incinerated with or without energy recovery or burned in cement kilns, 26% is mechanically recycled, 3.5% is chemically recycled and 7.4% is landfilled.

Heavy reliance on incinerating plastic waste and synthetic textiles in municipal solid waste results in GHG emissions of 4.7 million tonnes of CO₂, indicating that {{country}}'s key climate change mitigation action lies in reducing plastic waste generation.

environment.title: Plastics in the Environment
keyTrends: Plastics dominate coastal and beach litter in {{country}}. Total plastic leakage is relatively low but dominated by microplastics.
Macroplastic leakage (~1,900-7,500 tonnes per year) is driven by lightweight consumer plastics and fisheries losses. Leaked macroplastics are mainly PE, PP, and PET packaging and consumer items.
Microplastic leakage (11,000-24,000 tonnes per year) is dominated by tyre-wear and road marking particles.
environment.content:
Beach litter

The national average count of plastic beach litter items in {{country}} was 468,998 items per km² in 2018.

Plastic leakage trends

According to the Ministry of the Environment's study on Estimated Amount of Marine Plastic Litter Discharged in Japan conducted from 2024-2025, the estimated total amount of plastic discharged into the ocean from {{country}} is 13,000-31,000 tonnes per year.

Microplastics dominate total leakage by mass, accounting for 11,000-24,000 tonnes per year (80-85% of total leakage). Macroplastics from littering and lost fishing gear are much smaller in comparison (1,900-7,500 tonnes per year), due to the mature waste management and recycling systems in {{country}}. Macroplastic leakage is primarily composed of packaging and consumer items, largely PE, PP and PET.

The key sources of macroplastics include land-based littering, riverine transport, beach litter, and lost fishing gear. Although heavier rigid plastics dominate waste generation by mass, macroplastic leakage is skewed toward lightweight, mobile items (e.g., films, sheets, wrappers) that are easily transported by wind and rain and are less likely to be captured during mismanagement. ALDFG forms a separate and persistent macroplastic category, including nets, ropes, floats, and gear components.

Dominant sources of microplastic leakage are unintentionally added microplastics, including automotive tyre wear (5,700-9,400 tonnes per year) and road markings (3,600-4,300 tonnes per year), building and marine paints, synthetic textile fibres, and artificial turf. Intentionally added sources (460-3,400 tonnes per year) include resin pellets, agricultural coatings and additives, detergents and cosmetics.

Rainwater drainage is highlighted as a major pathway for microplastics, and, to a lesser extent, sewage systems, agricultural runoff, and waste disposal.

Removal trends

Annual macroplastic collection across beaches (~6,400 tonnes per year), rivers (~760-1,180 tonnes per year), and at sea (~700 tonnes per year) is comparable to estimated macroplastic leakage, highlighting that removal is essential but cannot substitute for upstream prevention, especially for microplastics.

sections.heading: 1. {{country}}’s plastics system is strongly upstream‑oriented, with production and trade dominated by resins and semi‑finished materials.
sections.text:
{{country}} produced 9.5 million tonnes of resins and 5.6 million tonnes of plastic products in 2022, with films, sheets, and packaging representing the largest share of output.

The country consistently maintains a large trade surplus, exporting USD 41–46 billion of plastics annually while importing only USD 25–30 billion.

Exports are heavily concentrated in primary polymers and semi-processed goods, confirming {{country}}’s role as a key supplier within Asian and global manufacturing chains.

sections.heading: 2. Plastic consumption is high and stable, reflecting entrenched demand across major sectors.
sections.text:
Total consumption estimates range from 9–12.8 million tonnes in 2022, depending on the methodologies used.

Construction (35%) and packaging (27%) dominate demand, highlighting long‑lived applications and strong reliance on single‑use systems.

Consumer products, textiles, transport, and electronics each contribute smaller but significant shares, indicating widespread inter-industry dependence.

sections.heading: 3. Waste management is heavily reliant on incineration, leading to substantial climate impacts and low material recovery.
sections.text:
{{country}} generates about 9 million tonnes of plastic waste per year, yet only ~14% is recycled (material recycling), with most of it incinerated, mostly with energy recovery, but some are without energy recovery.

MSW plastic waste alone amounts to 4.2 million tonnes, of which 68% is incinerated, and only 16% is mechanically recycled.

Incineration across MSW and non‑MSW streams produces 8.3 million tonnes of CO₂, marking plastic consumption as {{country}}’s clearest climate mitigation opportunity.

sections.heading: 4. Environmental plastic leakage is relatively low but dominated by fast‑moving microplastic sources.
sections.text:
Total leakage into the ocean is 13,000–31,000 tonnes per year, with microplastics accounting for 80–85% of the total by mass.

Tyre wear and road markings alone account for 9,300–13,700 tonnes of microplastics annually—{{country}}’s largest unintentional release pathway.

Macroplastic leakage remains comparatively small (1,900–7,500 tonnes) and consists mainly of lightweight packaging, consumer items, and lost fishing gear.
